label: Information Geometry
definition: The field that treats a family of probability distributions as points on a curved manifold, with the Fisher information as its natural metric. The reframing is powerful because it makes the difference between two distributions a geometric distance, which is what justifies the natural gradient and explains why ordinary parameter distance is the wrong ruler.

body.text: Information geometry takes a startling view: a family of probability distributions is a curved surface, and each particular distribution is a point on it. A Gaussian with a given mean and variance, a Bernoulli with a given bias, a softmax with given logits, each setting of the parameters is one point, and as the parameters vary continuously the points sweep out a manifold, the statistical manifold. The discipline studies the geometry of that surface, applying the machinery of differential geometry, distance, geodesics, curvature, to questions that look purely statistical.

The key move is choosing the right way to measure distance on this manifold, because the obvious choice is wrong. Ordinary Euclidean distance between parameter vectors does not reflect how different the distributions actually are: changing a Gaussian's mean by one unit matters enormously when its variance is tiny and barely at all when its variance is huge, so equal steps in parameter space are wildly unequal steps in distribution space. The natural, coordinate-independent metric is the Fisher information, which measures distance in terms of how statistically distinguishable two nearby distributions are. Under this metric, the squared distance between two infinitesimally close distributions equals their KL divergence, which is what ties the geometry directly to information theory.

This reframing pays off most concretely in optimization through the natural gradient. Ordinary gradient descent follows the steepest direction in parameter space, which depends on the arbitrary way the model happens to be parameterized and can take inefficient, zig-zagging paths. The natural gradient instead follows the steepest direction in distribution space, rescaling the gradient by the inverse Fisher information so that each step makes equal statistical progress regardless of parameterization. This is the principled version of what adaptive optimizers approximate, and trust-region methods in reinforcement learning rely on the same geometry to cap how far a policy distribution may shift per update.

Information geometry gives a unifying language for ideas scattered across machine learning. The KL divergence is revealed as a squared distance to second order, which is why it behaves like a discrepancy even though it is not symmetric and not literally a metric. Maximum likelihood becomes a projection onto the statistical manifold, and exponential families turn out to have an especially clean, nearly flat structure under the right connection. It is the deepest answer to a simple question: when you change a model's parameters, how far did the model itself actually move?

image.alt: A family of probability distributions forms a curved manifold on which a geodesic connects two distributions; labeled probability distribution, geodesic, and parameter.
image.caption: Information geometry treats a family of probability distributions as a curved surface, where each point is a distribution and the natural distance between nearby ones is the Fisher information. Recasting statistics as geometry explains why the straight line in parameter space is the wrong path and the geodesic on this manifold is the right one, the idea behind the natural gradient.
